RUNNING HEAD: NO SEMANTICS WITHOUT SPATIAL ATTENTION




---
title: "The Semantic Stroop Effect Requires Spatial Attention"
output: pdf_document
---



Serje Robidoux and Derek Besner

Department of Psychology 
Cognition and Perception Unit (CPU)
University of Waterloo, Canada

Word count:
Abstract: 173 words
Body: 3,343 words
References: 656 words










Funding for this project was provided by the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada [AO998] to DB. Correspondence should be addressed to Serje Robidoux, Psychology Department, University of Waterloo, Waterloo, ON, Canada, N2L 3G1. E-mail: smrobido@uwaterloo.ca, or dbesner@uwaterloo.ca

Abstract
There has been much debate about whether and to what extent word processing relies on spatial attention. The Stroop task has provided a popular method of testing this question. Results have varied, but in the experiments that most carefully control the deployment of spatial attention, evidence favours the view that the standard Stroop effect is eliminated if the colour-word is not attended (Robidoux & Besner, 2015). Other researchers have observed that the semantic Stroop effect (where the colour of a word like “lemon” is identified more quickly if the colour is consistent with the word [yellow] than if it is not [red]) is unaffected by manipulations that reduce the standard Stroop effect (the word “red” in red vs the word “red” in blue). Here we report an experiment that directly tests whether semantic processing of words is “automatic” in the sense of being capacity-free. Relying on Bayesian analysis techniques, we find that the evidence strongly favours the view that the semantic meanings of words are not accessed in the absence of spatial attention.

There is considerable debate in the visual word recognition literature about whether single word reading is “automatic”. Debate has centred on two dimensions of automaticity. The first is whether reading can be controlled (consciously or unconsciously) by the reader. This has been examined with single-letter colouring (sometimes spatially cueing the colored letter) to reduce the reliance on full-string processing (Manwell, Roberts, & Besner, 2004; Besner, Stolz, & Boutilier, 1997; Besner & Stolz, 1999; Augustinova, Flaudius, & Ferrand, 2010; Augustinova & Ferrand, 2012, 2014a, 2014b; Labuschagne & Besner, 2015), or through hypnotic suggestion (Raz & Campbell, 2011), or priming of dyslexia (Goldfarb, Aisenberg, & Henik, 2011). 
The other question addresses whether word identification requires spatial attention (i.e., all letters are homogenously colored; McCann, Folk & Johnston; Besner & Stolz, 1999; Brown, Gore, & Carr, 2002; Lachter, Forster, Ruthruff, 2004; Lachter, Ruthruff, Lien, & McCann, 2008; Waechter, Besner, & Stolz, 2011; Robidoux, Rauwerda, & Besner, 2014; Robidoux & Besner, 2015). It is this latter question that concerns us here. The history of this debate is more fully examined in Besner et al. (2015).
Arguments Favouring Automatic Semantic Processing
Augustinova et al. (2010) argued that single-letter colouring does not prevent the reading of the irrelevant word. Specifically, they coloured a single letter in colour-related words such as “lemon” presented in either a congruent (yellow) or incongruent colour (say, red), and asked participants to name the color of that single colored letter.  They reported a congruency effect such that responses were slower when the word was incongruent with the to be named colour than when it was congruent. This effect is typically described as a semantic Stroop effect for its similarity to the standard Stroop effect which use colour words that also appear in the verbal response set (such as “yellow”). Augustinova et al. further observed that the magnitude of the semantic Stroop effect was unaffected by conditions that typically reduced or eliminated congruency effects in the more typical color-words versions of the task. In recent years they have replicated the observation that the semantic Stroop effect persists when the standard Stroop effect is reduced (Augustinova & Ferrand, 2014a). Their argument is that reduction or elimination of Stroop effects using standard colour words does not necessarily mean that the distractor word has not been processed – rather, they reflect the elimination of response-competition components where, for example, the word “red” and the colour blue are each providing evidence for a different overt response. In semantic Stroop, the colour-related word “lemon” does not, itself, belong to the response set. Augustinova et al. (2010) make a strong claim that the semantic Stroop effect reflects automatic semantic activation (referred to as SA in the quote below), in the sense of being capacity free:
“…the fact that the semantically based Stroop effect is insensitive to manipulations designed to narrow attention seems to be consistent with the idea ‘that the amount of SA that a written word produces is not affected by concurrently operating attention-demanding mental operations’ (Neely & Kahan, 2001, p. 73) and can be thus considered to be capacity free.” (pp. 831-832, emphasis ours)

This claim (and others made by Augustinova & Ferrand, 2014b) imply that the semantic Stroop effect should persist, even if the distractor words are not spatially attended.
The spatial demands of Stroop effects have also been examined using segregated Stroop stimuli (where a word is presented in a different spatial location than the colour patch). This contrasts with the single-letter colouring paradigm used by Augustinova et al. (2010), and in other earlier research on the question (Manwell et al., 2004; Besner et al., 1997; Besner & Stolz, 1999). The single-letter paradigm is not as well-suited to debates about spatial attention, since the colour and word appear in the same spatial location. This makes it difficult to be certain that spatial attention is focused only on the coloured-letter on every trial (a problem Lachter et al., 2004, called “attentional slippage”; Labuschagne & Besner’s, 2015, experiment may overcome this problem, which we return to later). In an experiment that is more likely to keep attention away from the distractor word, Lachter et al. (2008; Experiment 3) used segregated Stroop stimuli (where the word and colour appear in different locations). They reported a small (4 ms), but persistent semantic Stroop effect.
Methodological Issues
The evidence for automatic semantic processing we have noted this far has come from Stroop tasks using single-letter colouring (Augustinova et al., 2010; Manwell et al, 2004), and segregated colour and word stimuli (Lachter et al., 2008). These studies each have limitations that undermine the conclusion that semantic processing is capacity-free, or does not rely on spatial attention. 
The Augustinova et al. (2010) studies do not strongly address the issue of spatial attention. Given that single-letter colouring does not separate the colour from the distractor word, it is difficult to be certain that spatial attention is restricted to the coloured letter, and does not take in the surrounding letters. Labuschagne and Besner (2015) modified the single letter colouring design to maximize readers’ ability to focus attention on the single coloured letter. By intermixing non-letter symbols with the letters of the words (e.g., f*r6o#g), they reasoned that any attentional slippage would only detect neighbouring symbols rather than word-relevant letters. They also blocked conditions, so that participants saw the all-letter-coloured trials before the single-letter coloured trials (or vice versa). This meant that participants could reliably focus attention narrowly during the block of single-letter coloured trials, while such careful deployment of attention wouldn’t be necessary for the all-letter coloured trials. With these two variations on the previous experiments, they found that the semantic Stroop effect, present when all letters shared the target colour, was eliminated when only a single letter is coloured and spatially cued. One limitation is that the study suffers from a form of circular argument: the lack of a semantic Stroop effect is taken as evidence that spatial attention was deployed only to the single coloured letter, which is then taken as evidence that spatial attention is required for semantic Stroop effects to appear.
An alternative method of ensuring spatial attention is on the colour alone, and not the distractor word, is to use a segregated Stroop task in which the target colour display and distractor words are presented in different spatial locations. Segregation does not eliminate the semantic Stroop effect either, however, as observed by Lachter et al. (2008). That study used a simple coloured rectangle for the colour displays. However, in their discussion of the standard Stroop effect using segregated stimuli, Waechter et al. (2011) proposed that simple colour displays may be too “easy” to identify in some sense, allowing a diffuse deployment of attention that would include the distractor words. Robidoux and Besner (2015; see also Robidoux et al., 2014) provided support for this hypothesis in a pair of experiments. They found that while standard Stroop effects persist when simple colour patches are used, a more complex colour patch eliminated the effect. Critically, the spatial attention demands of the more complex colour patch were independently assessed before being used in the Stroop task. In an exogenous cueing experiment (Posner, 1980), the complex patch was found to be more sensitive to the cued spatial attention than previous patches and as sensitive as word reading (Robidoux & Besner, 2015; Robidoux et al., 2014).
The Robidoux & Besner (2015) experiments made use of colour-words as the distractors, and thus they do not address the semantic Stroop effect. It remains possible that semantic processing is capacity free, and will persist when overt response competition is no longer part of the task demands. We therefore repeated Experiment 2 from Robidoux and Besner (2015), replacing the colour words with semantic associates. The question is whether a semantic Stroop effect will persist in a task that clearly eliminated a standard Stroop effect. Of critical importance to this study is that one theory predicts a true null – not simply a small or undetectable effect. Traditional frequentist statistical analyses are not well suited to this type of question, so Robidoux and Besner (2015) turned to Bayesian analyses to evaluate the evidence for and against a null effect. They found that the traditional colour patches overwhelmingly favoured the presence of a standard Stroop effect by more than 38,000 times. With a colour patch demonstrated to better capture spatial attention, the evidence clearly favoured the null by more than nine times. The same concerns are relevant here, and so we once again turn to Bayesian methods.
The Experiment
To test whether the semantic Stroop effect observed in Lachter et al. (2008) would persist using a colour patch that eliminates the standard Stroop effect where the distractor word appears in the response set, we adapted Robidoux and Besner’s (2015) experiment 2 to use colour-associated words as distractors rather than the colour words themselves. Methods and materials are identical to that experiment, except for the number of participants and the nature of the distractor words.
Method
Participants. As we are interested in the absence of an effect, we turn  to Bayesian analysis methods. Bayesian approaches eliminate concerns about stopping rules, allowing data to be collected until the experimenter is satisfied that the analysis favors either the presence or absence of a semantic Stroop effect. In the end, 14 participants were recruited through the University of Waterloo, with normal or corrected-to-normal vision, normal color vision, and whose first language was English. Participants received partial credit toward their courses or were paid CAD$7.50 for their participation. 
Design. The experiment consisted of a one factor (Congruency: congruent vs. incongruent) within-participants design, with reaction times (RTs) and accuracy as dependent variables.
Stimuli
Color patch: The colour patch is taken from Experiment 2 of Robidoux & Besner, 2015. It consists of a horizontal array of nine adjacent rectangles (without borders or gaps). Six of the rectangles are randomly selected to appear in the target colour (red, blue, green or yellow), while the remaining three rectangles appeared in a single distractor colour. The distractor colour was always one of the three remaining non-target colours. Each array extended 5 cm horizontally and 1 cm vertically (see Robidoux & Besner, 2015, for examples of this display).
Distractor words: the distractor words were associates of the four colours taken from Manwell, Roberts, & Besner (2004; sky for blue, frog for green, lemon for yellow, and tomato for red).
Procedure. Participants were individually seated at a comfortable distance from a computer monitor (approximately 50-60 cm). The trial sequence was designed to maximize the capture of attention. Initially, the screen consisted of an unfilled white rectangle on a black background, with two rows of six X’s (one above, and one below). After 750 ms the unfilled rectangle was filled with each of the four colours for 25 ms, in a random order to draw attention to the location of the target colour patch. Immediately after this 100 ms period, the unfilled rectangle was presented again along with the distractor word in either the top or bottom location (50% of trials in each location). Half of the time this distractor word was semantically related to the target colour, while on the other half the distractor word was semantically related to one of the two colours that did not appear in the display. That is, if the target stimulus was a mix of blue and yellow, then the incongruent distractor word could only be either “tomato” (red) or “frog” (green). The distractor word appeared for 50 ms, at which point both possible distractor locations were replaced with overlapping symbol strings (“%@&$#!” and “!#$%@&?”) acting as backward masks. Simultaneous with the onset of the masks, the target color patch appeared on the screen for 100 ms before being removed entirely. Participants were instructed to name the dominant color as quickly as possible (i.e., the color that occupied the majority of the display). Once a response was recorded, the screen displayed the target colour to allow the experimenter to code each response as correct, incorrect, or as a microphone misfire. The experimenter’s coding triggered the start of the next trial. Participants were instructed to keep their eyes on the rectangle in the center of the screen throughout each trial. 
For the experimental trials, each color appeared as the target 48 times, paired with each distractor color 16 times, and each target-distractor color pairing appeared equally often with congruent and incongruent distractor words. This provided a total of 192 experimental trials. The distractor word was randomly assigned on each trial to appear either above or below the target display with equal probability. The experimental trials were preceded by a block of 12 trials in which each colour combination was presented once, with an equal number of congruent and incongruent trials.
Stimuli were displayed on an LCD monitor with a 75Hz refresh rate controlled by E-Prime 2.0 software (Psychology Software Tools, Pittsburgh, PA). Response times were collected to the nearest millisecond.
Analysis Plan
We are directly comparing two theories about semantic Stroop. One theory argues that the effect reflects automatic and hence capacity-free processing (Augustinova et al., 2010; Augustinova & Ferrand, 2014b; Lachter et al., 2008; Brown et al., 2002), and thus that the semantic Stroop effect should persist when spatial attention is not directed to a distractor word. The other theory posits that word processing relies on spatial attention (Besner, Risko, & Sklair, 2005; Besner et al, 2015; Lachter et al., 2004; Waechter et al., 2011; Robidoux & Besner, 2015), and thus that the semantic Stroop effect should be eliminated when spatial attention is withheld from the distractor word. As one of these theories predicts a null effect, we adopt Bayesian statistical analysis procedures. In the Bayesian approach, two or more models are compared for their likelihoods given the data. In this case, we compare a model with Congruency effects included, to one in which Congruency effects are assumed to be zero. Data and analysis scripts can be found online at https://osf.io/9abw5/.
Results
In the first instance we removed microphone errors, resulting in the loss of 2.1% of trials. We then removed error trials (0.9% of trials; summarized in Table 1). We did not analyze accuracy since the error rates were so low.
RT transformation.
Since RTs are known to violate the regression assumptions of Gaussian residuals, we tested which of three transformations would best correct for the violations: untransformed RTs, a log normal transformation, or an inverse transformation (1/RT). Each of these DVs was fit to a full linear mixed effects model (using R package lme4, Bates, Maechler, Bolker, & Walker, 2015). The models shared the same structure: a fixed effect of Congruency, random intercepts for each participant and target colour, and random congruency slopes for each participant and target colour. In all cases, the model estimates were ignored, with only the residuals examined by visual inspection of normal quantile-quantile plots. From this inspection it was clear that the inverse transformations (1/RT) provided the best fits.
Outlier removal
After removing errors and misfires, outliers were removed by submitting the remaining inverse RTs to the recursive outlier analysis from Van Selst and Jolicoeur (1994). This removed 1.1% of the trials.
Reaction Times
The inverse RTs were used as DVs in two linear mixed effects models comparing the proposed theories. In the first model, Congruency effects are assumed to be present. Using R notation:
1/RT ~ Congruency + prevRT + (1 | Participant) + (1 | Colour)
This model includes random intercepts for both participants and the target colour (including random slopes for Congruency reduced the success of the model, and so they were omitted to maximize the chance of finding a Congruency effect). It also includes the reaction time on the previous trial as a control variable, since this has been found to considerably improve fits. The alternative model removed Congruency, leaving only previous RT in the fixed effects, and random intercepts for participants and target colours.
1/RT ~ prevRT + (1 | Participant) + (1 | Colour)
Bayesian priors
One concern with Bayesian analysis is that it relies on the setting of a prior for the magnitude of anticipated effects. If one sets the expected effect size too large, the results will unduly favour the null. For our purposes, the concern is how large a Congruency effect one might expect. Augustinova et al. (2010) reported semantic Stroop effects associated with Cohen’s d in the 0.8 to 1.4 range. In their experiments, the standard Stroop effect was robust and the stimuli differed from those used here. The most similar task to the present experiment (Lachter et al., 2008, experiment 3) produced an effect size of 0.7. Here again though, Lachter et al. (2008) found a standard Stroop effect with a Cohen’s d of 1.5.
Taking a conservative approach, we assumed the smallest semantic Stroop effect observed in previous studies examining this question (approximately 0.7). However, Bayes factors are sensitive to this choice, so we also analyse the data assuming an effect half that magnitude (0.35). This latter assumption may be more reasonable since Robidoux and Besner (2015) demonstrated that the standard Stroop effect is eliminated, not simply reduced as in the previous studies. It seems reasonable then, that any semantic Stroop effect that exists might be diminished relative to the previous demonstrations (though this would not be consistent with the strongest form of the capacity-free theory).
Bayes Factors
We used the BayesFactor package in R (Morey & Rouder, 2015) to calculate the Bayes factors. Bayes factors are the ratio of the evidence favoring one model over the other. For example, if we compare the null model to the model with a congruency effect, a Bayes Factor of 3 would imply that the data provide three times as much evidence for the null model as for the alternative model. A value of .25 would provide four times as much evidence for the alternative model. Though we prefer not to adopt strict rules about how much evidence should be necessary, rough guidelines have been suggested: values of 3 (or 1/3) provide “some” evidence in one direction or the other, while values of 10 (or 1/10) provide “strong” evidence. Since BayesFactor relies on Monte Carlo sampling, each run will produce different results. To avoid reporting one unusual result, we run the analysis 10 times and report the mean, median, and range of the resulting Bayes factors. Mean RTs (ms), mean inverse RTs (in items/second), and percentage errors appear in Table 1.


RT (ms)
%E
1000/RT
Incongruent
706
0.8
1.508
Congruent
707
0.9
1.500
Semantic Stroop Effect
-1 
-0.1
.008

Table 1. Means RTs, percentage errors, and mean inverse RTs in items/second for Experiment 1.

When specifying a prior effect size of 0.7, ten runs of the analysis produced a mean Bayes factor of 21.8 (median 21.8) favouring the null, ranging from 20.6 to 23.0. Using the smaller effect size (0.35) we find a mean Bayes factor of 10.8 (median 11.0), with a range of 9.7 to 11.6. That is, the data strongly favour the null model with no Congruency effect present, even when the prior allows for smaller effect sizes than have previously been reported. Note further that since the direction of the semantic Stroop effect is the reverse of the traditional effect (see Table 1), the increase in evidence for a Congruency effect when the prior assumes smaller effect sizes represents a decrease in evidence for the traditional (positive) Congruency effect. That said, the theory that processing is capacity-free does not itself explicitly predict one direction of effect over the other, only that there should be such an effect.
General Discussion and Conclusion
The automaticity of single word reading has been debated for some time. Stroop effects are often presented as evidence that word processing is automatic, in the sense that readers are unable to prevent it (Lachter et al., 2008; Augustinova et al., 2010; Augustinova & Ferrand, 2014ab; Brown et al., 2002; Rayner & Pollatsek, 1989; including most undergraduate cognition textbooks, e.g., Ashcraft, 1994). Stronger claims have been that word processing is also capacity-free - it does not require any form of attention. Though Robidoux and Besner (2015) found that when spatial attention is appropriately controlled, the standard Stroop effect using colors and color words (RED presented in red vs. BLUE presented in red) is eliminated, other researchers (Augustinova et al., 2010; Augustinova & Ferrand, 2014a; 2014b) have argued that semantic Stroop effects persist under conditions that reduce or eliminate standard Stroop effects. Here we extend Robidoux and Besner’s result and demonstrate unequivocally that for spatial attention, this is not the case. Taken together with the results from Robidoux and Besner (2015), these data provide the clearest evidence to date that if words are not spatially attended, then they are not processed.

References
Ashcraft, M. H. (1994). Human Memory and Cognition. New York, NY: Harper Collins.
Augustinova, M., & Ferrand, L. (2012). Suggestion does not de-automatize word reading: Evidence from the semantically based Stroop task. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 19(3), 521-527.
Augustinova, M., & Ferrand, L. (2014a). Social priming of dyslexia and reduction of the Stroop effect: What component of the Stroop effect is actually reduced?. Cognition, 130(3), 442-454.
Augustinova, M., & Ferrand, L. (2014b). Automaticity of word reading: evidence from the semantic Stroop paradigm. Current Direcions in Psychological Science 23, 343–348. doi: 10.1177/0963721414540169
Augustinova, M., Flaudias, V., & Ferrand, L. (2010). Single-letter coloring and spatial cuing do not eliminate or reduce a semantic contribution to the Stroop effect. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review. 17, 827–833. doi: 10.3758/PBR.17.6.827
Bates, D., Maechler, M., Bolker, B., & Walker, S. (2015). Fitting Linear Mixed-Effects Models Using lme4. Journal of Statistical Software, 67(1), 1-48. doi:10.18637/jss.v067.i01.
Besner, D., Risko, E. F., Stolz, J. A., White, D., Reynolds, M., O’Malley, S., & Robidoux, S. (2016). Varieties of Attention: Their Roles in Visual Word Identification. Current Directions in Psychological Science, 25(3), 162-168.
Besner, D., Risko, E. F., & Sklair, N. (2005). Spatial attention as a necessary preliminary to early processes in reading. Canadian Journal of Experimental Psychology/Revue canadienne de psychologie expérimentale, 59(2), 99.
Besner, D., & Stolz, J. A. (1999). What kind of attention modulates the Stroop effect? Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 6, 99–104. doi: 10.3758/BF03210815
Besner, D., Stolz, J. A., & Boutilier, C. (1997). The Stroop effect and the myth of automaticity. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 4, 221–225. doi: 10.3758/BF03209396
Brown, T. L., Gore, C. L., & Carr, T. H. (2002). Visual attention and word recognition in Stroop color naming: is word recognition “automatic”? Journal of Experimental Psychology, 131, 220–240. doi: 10.1037/0096-3445.131.2.220
Goldfarb, L., Aisenberg, D., & Henik, A. (2011). Think the thought, walk the walk–Social priming reduces the Stroop effect. Cognition, 118(2), 193-200.
Labuschagne, E. M., & Besner, D. (2015). Automaticity revisited: when print doesn't activate semantics. Frontiers in Psychology, 6. doi: 10.3389/fpsyg.2015.00117
Lachter, J., Forster, K. I., & Ruthruff, E. (2004). Forty-five years after Broadbent (1958): still no identification without attention. Psychological Review, 11, 880–913. doi: 10.1037/0033-295X.111.4.880
Lachter, J., Ruthruff, E., Lien, M., & McCann, R. S. (2008). Is attention needed for word identification? Evidence from the Stroop paradigm. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 15, 950–955. doi: 10.3758/PBR.15.5.950
Manwell, L. A., Roberts, M. A., & Besner, D. (2004). Single letter coloring and spatial cuing eliminates a semantic contribution to the Stroop effect. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 11, 458–462. doi: 10.3758/BF03196595
McCann, R. S., Folk, C. L., & Johnston, J. C. (1992). The role of spatial attention in visual word processing. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance, 18(4), 1015.
Morey, R.D & Rouder, J.N. (2015). BayesFactor: Computation of Bayes Factors for Common Designs. R package version 0.9.12-2. https://CRAN.R‑project.org/package=BayesFactor
Posner, M. I. (1980). Orienting of attention. Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology, 32 (1), 3–25. doi:10.1080/00335558008248231.
Rayner, K., & Pollatsek, A. (1989). The Psychology of Reading. Englewood Cliffs, NJ: Prentice Hall.
Raz, A., & Campbell, N. K. (2011). Can suggestion obviate reading? Supplementing primary Stroop evidence with exploratory negative priming analyses. Consciousness and Cognition, 20(2), 312-320.
Robidoux, S. & Besner, D. (2015). Conflict resolved: On the role of spatial attention in reading and color naming tasks. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 22(6). 1709-1716. doi:10.3758/s13423-015-0830-7
Robidoux, S., Rauwerda, D., & Besner, D. (2014). Basic processes in reading aloud and colour naming: towards a better understanding of the role of spatial attention. Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology, 67, 979–990. doi: 10.1080/17470218.2013.838686
Van Selst, M., & Jolicoeur, P. (1994). A solution to the effect of sample size on outlier estimation. Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology, 47, 631–650. doi: 10.1080/14640749408401131
Waechter, S., Besner, D., & Stolz, J.A. (2011): Basic processes in reading: Spatial attention as a necessary preliminary to orthographic and semantic processing, Visual Cognition, 19(2), 171-202. doi:10.1080/13506285.2010.517228
